sub: The message formats aviation actually speaks

title: The Aviation Message Zoo
desc: Five formats you will meet in every air traffic system

rows: Time-critical notices: closed runways, GPS outages, airspace restrictions
Coded weather observation at an aerodrome, typically every 30–60 min
Structured aeronautical data (airports, airspaces, routes, navaids) as an XML data model
All-purpose STructured Eurocontrol suRveillance Information eXchange
Binary surveillance data: radar plots, system tracks (e.g. CAT062)
Coordination messages between air traffic control centres

title: NOTAM: Notice to Airmen
desc: A real NOTAM — GPS unreliable over southern Switzerland

body.code:
LSAZ/ZURICH AREA A0195 03MAY21 LSAZ
B) 04JUL210730  C) 04DEZ311159  D) PERM
E) GPS SIGNAL UNREL FOR NAV WI SWITZERLAND
   S OF 4605N (S OF MONTE CENERI)
F) GND  G) FL150
body.note: Why should a drone system care? A GPS-unreliable zone is exactly the kind of constraint an unmanned traffic system must ingest automatically — no human pilot reads NOTAMs for a delivery drone.

items: GPS signal unreliable for navigation within Switzerland, south of 46°05'N (south of Monte Ceneri)
From ground level (GND) up to flight level 150 (FL150 ≈ 15,000 ft)
Valid 21 July 2004 07:30 UTC until 31 December 2004 11:59 UTC — permanent within that window

rows: Validity within the FIR (flight information region); here: Zurich
Area of validity: a FIR or an aerodrome
Schedule within the validity — permanent, or e.g. 'MON–FRI 0800–1200'
The actual information, in heavily abbreviated aviation English

title: METAR: Aviation Weather Reports
desc: One line tells a pilot — or a drone — everything about the weather at Stuttgart

rows: Observation time: 8th of the month, 16:20 UTC ('Zulu')
Few clouds (1–2 oktas) at 4,000 ft above ground, type: towering cumulus
QNH 1012 hPa — pressure reduced to sea level
'No significant change' expected in the next two hours

title: AIXM: Aeronautical Information Exchange Model
desc: The standardized data model behind aeronautical information

items: A standardized data model to describe and exchange aeronautically relevant information
Enables standardized communication of airports, flight routes, airspaces and more
Widely used in aeronautical information processing and air traffic management
Technically: a UML model + XML/GML schema — temporality built in (features change validity over time)
Airport information — ICAO code, position, runways
Airspace structures — control zones, airway routes
Flight plan data — planned routes and altitudes
Weather information — meteorological reports for air traffic
Navigation aids — VOR, NDB

title: AIXM in Practice: A Runway as Data
desc: A (shortened) AIXM feature — note the identifiers and validity periods

body.note: Every feature is versioned in time (timeSlice) — the model answers not only 'what is runway 25?' but 'what was runway 25 valid on a given date?'. Exactly what a UTM system needs for reproducible decisions.

title: ASTERIX: Surveillance Data Exchange
desc: The binary format your radar tracks arrive in

body.alt: ASTERIX protocol analyzer decoding CAT062 system track records

items: Eurocontrol's binary format family for surveillance data — organized in categories (e.g. CAT048 radar target reports, CAT062 system tracks)
Each record carries data items like position (WGS-84), track velocity, mode 3/A code, flight level
The ASTERIX toolkit shown above decodes, analyzes, records and replays this data
Compact and fast — but you need the category specification to read a single bit

title: OLDI: On-Line Data Interchange
desc: How air traffic control centres coordinate a flight hand-over

items: Standardized messages exchanged between flight data processing systems of adjacent ATC units
Replaces telephone coordination for routine hand-overs — faster, less error-prone
Typical messages: ABI (advance boundary information), ACT (activation), LAM (logical acknowledgement)
Defined by the EUROCONTROL OLDI specification; mandatory for many European centre pairs
UTM systems will need the same pattern: machine-to-machine coordination when a drone crosses from one service provider's area to another
The lesson from OLDI: agree the message semantics first, or every border becomes a phone call
Aviation exchanges information in standardized, machine-readable formats — some 100 years old in spirit (NOTAM, METAR), some modern XML models (AIXM), some binary (ASTERIX)
NOTAM/METAR: compact coded text — you can now decode both by hand
AIXM: temporal XML data model for everything static in the airspace
ASTERIX: binary surveillance categories — the live positions
OLDI: coordination between control centres — the pattern UTM must reinvent for drones
Any serious drone traffic system is, at its heart, an integrator of these formats
